description: Kneel on throwing-arm-side knee, hold plyo ball low, counter-rotate the torso and drive the arm back through the slot to throw against a wall behind you. Trains the posterior shoulder and scap to accept high-velocity deceleration force plus T-spine mobility. Always the FIRST drill in the Driveline plyo routine.
notes: First drill in the Driveline plyo-ball throwing-program sequence; ball weight 450g/225g (~16/8oz plyo, soft, sub-max intent). relevance: builds posterior-shoulder/scap durability that lets the arm whip instead of push off a braced front side

description: Start side-on; counter-rotate and hang the throwing hand back, then drive the back hip and wrist over the elbow into the wall. Trains forward rotation sequencing, medial-forearm strength, and pronation timing. Performed early in the plyo routine after reverse throws.
notes: Throwing-program sequence member; 1kg green + 450g blue plyo. Forces back-hip drive before arm — directly targets the drop-to-rotate failure. relevance: trains back-hip rotation/clearing so the trail hip stops leaking energy

description: Slow-motion / high-speed video breakdown of the pivot pickoff — counter-rotate, hang the hand, fire the hips, drive wrist over elbow. Visual reference for the rotation-then-arm sequence.
notes: Visual companion to the Pivot Pickoff drill row. relevance: shows the back-hip-drive-before-arm timing Landon is missing in the drop-to-rotate transition

description: Take walking steps with toes pointed at the target while MINIMIZING hip rotation; the throwing arm drives straight back as the stride foot contacts, then throw. Constrains the lower half to keep the front side closed and arm back.
notes: Throwing-program sequence member; 450g blue + 225g red plyo. Keeps toes/front side closed while loading the arm. relevance: trains staying closed and a delayed front side — the headline fix for early front-hip open

description: Set feet wider than stride with front foot slightly closed to the target; rock back and forth 1-3 times to load the back leg and create rhythm, then throw to a target. Trains weight shift, back-leg loading, and front-leg strength. Prescribed a few weeks in after other mechanics clean up.
notes: Throwing-program sequence member; finishes in a delivery-like position. relevance: trains back-hip load-and-drive and weight shift into a braced front leg (lead-leg block)

description: Step back with the back leg, gather into a coil keeping the back hip externally rotated and the outside of the foot anchored, then throw. Anchors the back leg and improves glute/hamstring activation into the drive.
notes: Throwing-program sequence member; precursor to Walking Windups. relevance: anchors back hip in external rotation so it can rotate/clear instead of leaking

description: A walker step into the leg kick using momentum directed at the target, progressing from drop-step throws into a full-delivery feel. LAST drill in the Driveline plyo routine.
notes: Final drill in the plyo-ball throwing-program sequence; blends pattern toward the mound. relevance: integrates staying-closed + momentum into a delivery-like pattern before transfer

description: Face the target, take one step back with the drive leg, then step forward to throw. Simplifies the load so the athlete can feel back-leg engagement before adding momentum.
notes: Constraint-drill family; entry-level back-leg loader. relevance: isolates trail-leg drive feeding the hip rotation Landon stalls on

description: Constraint drill (studied in Driveline's drill-kinematics work) that constrains the lower half/arm path to feel sequencing — counter-rotation and delayed trunk rotation while the arm works back.
notes: Constraint-drill family used in Driveline drill-constraint kinematics studies. relevance: trains delayed trunk rotation / staying closed while hips lead

equipment: Plyo Balls (450g-2kg heavy, 150g-450g medium, 100g-225g light), wall
description: Three-round constraint warmup: Round 1 heavy balls (450g-2kg) at 25-50% intent, Round 2 medium (150g-450g) at 50-75%, Round 3 lightest (100g-225g) at 50-75%. Sub-max constraint work every throwing day; never max intent.
notes: Daily sub-max constraint routine; lightest 100g ball ~3.5oz underload CNS work is Landon-appropriate. 2kg/1kg heavy plyos are HEAVY but used at low intent for pattern, not max-intent overload. relevance: low-stress daily pattern work for staying closed + arm path without medial-elbow load

description: Lower-effort plyo throws at 50-60% intensity on recovery days; light balls only. Maintains pattern and blood flow without adding stress.
notes: Recovery-day variant of the plyo routine; ~3.5oz light balls. relevance: conservative-load CNS/pattern maintenance fitting Landon's flexor-pronator history

description: Medium-effort plyo throwing day at ~70% intent (Hybrid B). Adds force to the constraint patterns without reaching max intent.
notes: Hybrid B = 70% intent. Acceptable mid-progression intensity. relevance: graded force progression onto the staying-closed/rotation patterns once base is built

description: High-effort plyo throwing day reaching up to 90% intent (Hybrid A). Highest-intent plyo day short of true velocity days.
notes: Hybrid A reaches ~90% intent — year-2 / not-now for Landon's conservative year; defer sustained 90% intent to Phase 6+. relevance: high-intent transfer of pattern, hold until medial elbow proven durable

description: Driveline video walking through the plyocare drill set (reverse throws, pivot pickoffs, roll-ins, rockers) with technique cues for each.
notes: Video index of the plyocare drill family. relevance: visual reference for the constraint drills that train staying closed + arm path

description: Driveline instructional video demonstrating plyo ball drill mechanics and routine order.
notes: Video reference for plyo routine order. relevance: shows sequencing of staying-closed constraint drills

description: Driveline in-season pre-game routine using plyo balls to prime the arm and pattern before competition at controlled intent.
notes: In-season application of the plyo routine. relevance: low-stress pattern priming that keeps the front-side-closed feel without adding load

description: Driveline breakdown of the towel drill, position holds, and the up-down-out arm-action pattern — feeling the arm path and layback positions without throwing a ball.
notes: Constraint/feel work for arm action. relevance: grooves a whip/spiral arm path and layback to replace the push arm action

description: Article: the stride/lead leg must 'slam on the brakes' applying posterior force to stop momentum and catapult the body and ball toward the plate. Lead-leg posterior force correlates with velocity (back-leg drive does not). Explains the brace that converts hip drive into arm whip.
notes: Conceptual/diagnostic basis for the lead-leg block. relevance: THE article on the lead-leg block — the single most important feature for Landon (converts back-hip drive into a braced front side that whips the arm)

drill_or_video_name: Analyzing Front and Back Leg Ground Forces
description: Research article: stride-leg ground reaction force during arm-cocking/acceleration strongly correlates with ball velocity (r-squared 0.45-0.61); back-leg 'drive' does NOT significantly correlate. Peak stride-leg force in arm-cocking is the best velocity predictor.
notes: Evidence base for prioritizing the lead-leg block over back-leg push. relevance: quantifies why fixing Landon's front-side brace (not just back-leg drive) is what unlocks velocity

drill_or_video_name: A Different Model for Cueing Pitching Mechanics
description: Article: minimize internal cues, prefer external-focus cues, and use the plyo-ball wall to separate movement work from throwing. To get hip-shoulder separation the hips must rotate open while the trunk stays closed at footstrike; anti-rotation core stability enables it.
notes: Coaching framework for the separation cue. relevance: defines the staying-closed mechanism (hips open, trunk closed at footstrike) that Landon loses when shoulders fly open

description: Article: overhead ROM depends on scapular upward rotation and T-spine extension; priorities are pain-free arm-overhead, sufficient GH internal/external rotation, and open hips to create hip-to-scap separation.
notes: Mobility prerequisites for separation. relevance: identifies the T-spine/hip mobility Landon needs to keep the trunk neutral while hips rotate

description: Article: anti-rotation core stability lets athletes keep the trunk closed while the lower half and hips rotate open — the stability side of hip-shoulder separation.
notes: Stability prerequisite for separation. relevance: anti-rotation trunk stability is what stops Landon's shoulders flying open at landing

drill_or_video_name: Why We Don't Teach Equal and Opposite / Firm Front Side
description: Article: a glove-blocking 'firm front side' that stops deceleration short kills shoulder angular rotational velocity and adds shoulder/neck stress. The lead leg blocks but the glove side should not jam — nuance on front-side discipline.
notes: Nuance: lead-LEG block yes, glove-jam no. relevance: refines Landon's front-side fix — block with the leg, keep the glove/trunk discipline without over-blocking

description: Article: how Driveline reads a biomech report — staying stacked/closed (negative trunk angle/tilt at foot plant) can produce ~48 degrees hip-shoulder separation (elite); separation ties to trunk rotation velocity and pitch velocity.
notes: Diagnostic frame for separation metrics. relevance: gives the target (stacked/closed at foot plant -> big separation) that Landon's early-open pattern fails to hit

drill_or_video_name: Workload, Range of Motion, and Early Season Injuries
description: Research article linking mismanaged acute:chronic workload and ROM loss to early-season injuries; effects can be delayed. Workload monitoring rationale.
notes: Workload-injury research. relevance: workload/ROM monitoring discipline is critical given Landon's flexor-pronator history and conservative-load year

drill_or_video_name: Why We Are Changing Our Recovery Protocols
description: Article: Driveline's revamped recovery — EMS lowered blood lactate and gave best self-reported recovery; tiered recovery (traditional for low-intensity/volume days, revamped protocol after moderate-to-high intensity/volume days).
notes: Recovery protocol rationale. relevance: tiered post-throw recovery protects Landon's medial elbow during the build

drill_or_video_name: Comparing Overload vs. Underload Weighted Ball Stress
description: Research article: insufficient evidence that under- or over-load implements are more stressful than a 5oz ball at max effort. Underload moves the arm faster with less force; overload slower with more force. Driveline rarely exceeds 9oz overload, never below 3oz underload.
notes: year-2 / not-now for the overload (7-9oz) side. Underload 3-4oz is Landon-appropriate. relevance: justifies using 3-4oz underload for arm speed while deferring 7oz+ overload due to flexor-pronator history

description: Open-access peer-reviewed study (PeerJ 2018, PMC6254244): 17 collegiate/pro pitchers, 6 weeks of weighted-implement throwing. Internal-rotation velocity rose significantly; no group velocity change; documents arm-stress/ROM changes.
notes: year-2 / not-now — protocol involves max-intent weighted-ball throwing and 2-7oz implements. relevance: evidence base for weighted-ball arm-speed work; informs a conservative version (underload only) for Landon

drill_or_video_name: Range of Motion, Hip-Shoulder Separation & Kinematics (study)
description: Open-access study (IJSPT, PMC7727427): hip-shoulder separation at foot contact, peak pelvis velocity, and the timing of peak pelvis velocity each individually predict peak trunk velocity; peak trunk velocity predicts ball velocity. Pelvis peaks ~12% of stride-to-release, trunk ~36%.
notes: Core kinematic-sequencing evidence (pelvis->trunk->arm timing). relevance: explains EXACTLY Landon's problem — separation at foot contact + pelvis-leading-trunk timing drives velocity; his early-open pattern collapses this sequence

drill_or_video_name: Spin Rate Part II: Spin Axis & Useful Spin
description: Article: a stable spin axis and high useful/true spin are the most vital pieces of a pitcher's effectiveness; defines spin axis and useful spin.
notes: Pitch-design fundamentals. relevance: secondary goal — release-off-the-fingers and spin axis for refining Landon's 4-seam/slider once mechanics improve

drill_or_video_name: Mastering the Axis of Rotation (Spin Axis 3D)
description: Thorough review of spin axis in three dimensions and spin efficiency — what percentage of imparted spin produces movement, and how coaches use it for pitch design.
notes: Deep spin-axis / pitch-design reference. relevance: supports release/finger-drive refinement and slider shape (P4, secondary for Landon)

drill_or_video_name: Optimizing Breaking Ball Shape (Data-Driven Pitch Design)
description: Article: tradeoffs between adding sweep (lose velo) vs adding velo (lose depth/spin efficiency) when designing a breaking ball; data-driven slider/cutter shaping.
notes: Slider/breaking-ball design. relevance: directly applicable to refining Landon's slider via release and spin efficiency (secondary goal)

description: Article on velocity-based training in the weight room — using bar-speed feedback to autoregulate power/strength work that underpins throwing velocity.
notes: Weight-room velocity development; Landon is an advanced lifter so this is high-leverage. relevance: builds the power base for arm speed without throwing-side medial-elbow stress

description: Article: measuring elbow/UCL stress with the PULSE sleeve and how it scales with pitching velocity — workload/torque monitoring concept.
notes: Elbow-stress monitoring rationale. relevance: directly relevant to protecting Landon's medial elbow — track torque as velocity rises in the build
